prose:
**Fukui reactivity analysis of phenol** — DFT, tier `standard` (**B3LYP/def2-TZVP**, PySCF, density-fit by default), **gas phase** (no solvent model), neutral reference state charge 0, multiplicity 1. Partial-charge scheme: **Mulliken (PySCF)**. The finite-difference Fukui functions were built from three states: neutral (charge 0, mult 1), cation (charge +1, mult 2), and anion (charge −1, mult 2). Geometry provenance: the user-supplied file `/home/erobles/chem-skills/benchmarks/fidelity/fukui-reactivity-validation/phenol/phenol.xyz` (13 atoms), used as-is — this task performs single-point calculations at that fixed geometry (no relaxation at DFT).

**Headline result** — the **most electrophilic site** (largest f⁺, susceptible to nucleophilic attack) is **atom 3 (C), f⁺ = 2.1028319** (0-based index 2). The **most nucleophilic site** (largest f⁻, susceptible to electrophilic attack) is **atom 1 (O), f⁻ = 0.16201477** (0-based index 0). Note these Fukui values are condensed-to-atom, Mulliken-population-based finite-difference descriptors and are dimensionless; they inherit the known sensitivity of Mulliken populations to basis set (the anion/cation Mulliken charges here show large, physically exaggerated values typical of diffuse anionic densities in a large basis).

**Method used:** DFT B3LYP/def2-TZVP (tier `standard`), gas phase, via PySCF (`dft` backend). Command: `.../cli.py fukui --method dft --functional b3lyp --basis def2-tzvp --tier standard .../phenol.xyz --out .../live.json`.

**Integrity:** the engine reports `status: ok`, `trustworthy: true`.
